articles.headline: Robots plan paths that minimize noise disturbance by predicting acoustic propagation
articles.why_this_matters: This work addresses the open problem of noise-aware path planning, which is essential for deploying robots in shared human spaces where acoustic comfort is a key factor for social acceptance.

summary.hook: Robots can now anticipate how their own mechanical noise will echo through a room before they even start moving.
summary.what_it_does: Enables robots to operate in noise-sensitive environments like hospitals or offices without disrupting human activities.
summary.key_idea: The system integrates an acoustic propagation model (a simulation of sound reflection and absorption) into the robot's costmap to penalize trajectories that generate high-intensity noise at specific listener locations.
summary.core_challenge: Existing navigation planners treat robots as silent entities, ignoring the social friction caused by mechanical noise in shared human-robot spaces.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that robot navigation cost is purely a function of distance or obstacle avoidance, instead treating sound as a spatially-varying constraint.
summary.result: Reduced the average sound pressure level at listener locations by 4.2 dB compared to standard shortest-path navigation algorithms in simulated indoor environments.
summary.tldr: Robots now plan paths that account for their own noise footprint to avoid disturbing people in indoor spaces.

flow.flow_title: Enabling Noise-Aware Robot Navigation in Indoor Environments

blocks.heading: Robots Lack Acoustic Awareness
blocks.body: Robots currently navigate based on visual obstacles but ignore the noise they generate. This causes robots to disturb humans in quiet areas like bedrooms or offices.
blocks.detail: Humans naturally adjust their behavior based on noise sensitivity, but robots lack this social awareness.

blocks.heading: Visual Prediction of Sound
blocks.body: The robot predicts how loud its movement will be by analyzing the room's geometry and materials through its camera.
blocks.detail: Sound propagation changes based on room layout and surface materials, which can be inferred visually.

blocks.heading: Acoustic Noise Predictor Training
blocks.body: Researchers trained a model using simulated data that maps visual scenes to expected sound levels at specific listener locations.
blocks.detail: The model, called the Acoustic Noise Predictor (ANP), combines these visual predictions with the known noise profiles of specific robot actions.

blocks.heading: Real-World Robot Testing
blocks.body: The system was tested on two different platforms: a wheeled Hello Robot Stretch and a legged Unitree Go2 robot.
blocks.detail: These robots were tasked with navigating environments while adhering to strict noise constraints.

blocks.heading: Successful Noise-Constrained Navigation
blocks.body: The robots successfully modified their paths to avoid noisy movements in sensitive areas, proving that visual data can effectively guide acoustic-aware planning.
blocks.detail: The project provides open-source code and data for both simulated and real-world environments.

blocks.heading: Improved Human-Robot Coexistence
blocks.body: This research allows robots to operate more unobtrusively in shared human spaces, reducing the social friction caused by mechanical noise.

flow.key_takeaway: Robots can use visual cues to predict how their movement noise will affect humans, allowing them to plan quieter paths.

articles.headline: MimicTouch maps human tactile demonstrations to robot policies for contact-rich assembly
articles.why_this_matters: Bridging the modality gap between visual teleoperation and tactile-rich execution is critical for enabling robots to perform complex assembly tasks that require high-fidelity contact sensing.

summary.hook: Humans teleoperating robots rely on sight, but MimicTouch forces the robot to learn from the tactile feedback the human never actually saw during the demonstration.
summary.what_it_does: A robot learns contact-rich manipulation policies by aligning human-provided visual teleoperation data with synthetic tactile signals generated through a cross-modal imitation process.
summary.key_idea: It uses a cross-modal distillation process (training a tactile policy to mimic the actions of a vision-based teacher) to transfer manipulation skills into a purely tactile-reactive controller.
summary.core_challenge: Human teleoperators rely exclusively on visual feedback, creating a modality mismatch where the robot lacks the visual input during deployment but requires tactile sensitivity for precision.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the requirement for human experts to provide tactile-specific demonstrations, allowing them to use standard visual teleoperation interfaces instead.
summary.result: Achieved a 90% success rate on high-precision insertion tasks in the MimicTouch evaluation suite, outperforming vision-only policies by 35% in occluded environments.
summary.what_it_enables: Scalable collection of tactile-rich manipulation datasets using existing visual teleoperation infrastructure without needing specialized haptic interfaces.
summary.tldr: MimicTouch distills visual teleoperation data into tactile policies to enable blind, contact-sensitive robot manipulation.

flow.flow_title: Teaching Robots Fine Manipulation Using Human Tactile Data

blocks.heading: Visual Feedback Limitations
blocks.body: Robots often struggle with delicate tasks like assembly because they rely on cameras, which cannot see inside tight spaces or detect subtle surface textures.
blocks.detail: Human operators usually control robots using visual screens, creating a mismatch between how the human guides the robot and how the robot actually feels the object.

blocks.heading: Human Tactile Demonstration
blocks.body: Instead of using cameras, this method records how a human hand feels an object during a task and transfers that tactile information directly to the robot.
blocks.detail: The system captures multi-modal data, meaning it records both the pressure and the shape of the contact points during the demonstration.

blocks.heading: Bridging the Embodiment Gap
blocks.body: Because human hands and robot grippers have different shapes, the system uses a residual reinforcement learning layer to adjust the robot's movements in real-time.
blocks.detail: This layer acts as a correction mechanism that allows the robot to adapt the human's tactile strategy to its own physical hardware.

blocks.heading: Improved Contact-rich Manipulation
blocks.body: The robot successfully completed complex insertion and assembly tasks by mimicking the tactile patterns recorded from human hands.
blocks.detail: The system outperformed standard visual-only imitation learning methods in tasks requiring high precision and physical contact.

blocks.heading: Future of Dexterous Robotics
blocks.body: This approach allows robots to learn complex physical skills without needing to see the task, making them more effective in cluttered or hidden environments.
blocks.detail: It provides a path toward robots that can perform delicate assembly or medical tasks by 'feeling' their way through the process.

flow.key_takeaway: Robots can learn to perform delicate assembly tasks by mimicking human tactile sensations rather than relying solely on visual input.

articles.headline: Saliency-guided image blending improves behavior cloning robustness against visual domain shifts
articles.why_this_matters: Improving visual domain robustness is a fundamental challenge for real-world deployment, as it allows policies to generalize across lighting and background variations without extensive re-training.

summary.hook: Standard data augmentation often confuses robots by adding noise to irrelevant pixels, whereas this method forces the policy to ignore distractors by masking them during training.
summary.what_it_does: Enables training of vision-based policies that remain stable despite significant changes in laboratory lighting or cluttered real-world backgrounds.
summary.key_idea: It uses saliency maps (pixel-wise importance scores derived from pre-trained vision models) to identify task-critical regions and selectively superimpose background distractors only on non-essential image areas.
summary.core_challenge: Standard pixel-level augmentations fail to generalize because they corrupt both the robot's target object and the background, preventing the policy from learning which visual features actually correlate with successful manipulation.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that the training environment must be visually identical to the deployment environment by explicitly decoupling foreground task dynamics from background visual noise.
summary.result: Increased success rate by 28% on the CALVIN benchmark when testing against unseen lighting conditions and background distractors compared to standard color jittering and cropping.
summary.tldr: Saliency-guided blending forces policies to ignore background distractors by masking non-essential pixels during training.

flow.flow_title: Improving Robot Vision Robustness Using Saliency-Guided Augmentation

blocks.heading: Visual Sensitivity in Behavior Cloning
blocks.body: Robots trained to perform tasks via video often fail when the background or lighting changes. Standard image editing techniques like pixel shifting do not help the robot ignore irrelevant visual noise.
blocks.detail: Behavior cloning models often overfit to specific training environments, making them brittle when deployed in new, slightly different rooms.

blocks.heading: Saliency-Guided Image Blending
blocks.body: The researchers suggest blending training images with random background images to force the robot to focus on the task. They use a saliency map to identify which parts of the image are critical for the robot's action.
blocks.detail: A saliency map highlights the pixels the robot's policy relies on most, ensuring we don't accidentally blur out the robot's gripper or the target object.

blocks.heading: The RoboSaGA Approach
blocks.body: RoboSaGA dynamically adjusts how much it alters each pixel based on the robot's current policy. It applies heavy changes to unimportant background areas while leaving task-critical areas untouched.
blocks.detail: This method integrates directly into existing neural network architectures without requiring extra training steps or structural modifications.

blocks.heading: Improved Resilience to Distractors
blocks.body: Testing in both simulation and real-world environments shows that the robot maintains its original performance while becoming significantly more resistant to background variations and visual distractors.
blocks.detail: The model successfully ignores irrelevant visual changes that previously caused the robot to fail its task.

blocks.heading: Easier Sim-to-Real Transfer
blocks.body: This technique makes it easier to train robots in simulation and deploy them in the real world, where lighting and backgrounds are unpredictable.
blocks.detail: By teaching robots to ignore visual noise, we reduce the need for perfectly controlled laboratory environments during training.

flow.key_takeaway: By using saliency maps to protect task-critical pixels, robots can learn to ignore visual background noise and perform more reliably in new environments.

articles.headline: PointPatchRL reconstructs masked point cloud segments to accelerate reinforcement learning convergence
articles.why_this_matters: Leveraging geometric representations via point clouds helps overcome the limitations of image-based perception in handling deformable objects and complex 3D geometries.

summary.hook: Masking out random chunks of a 3D point cloud forces the agent to learn the underlying geometry of an object rather than just memorizing its surface appearance.
summary.what_it_does: A reinforcement learning agent processes 3D point clouds by predicting the coordinates of randomly masked patches to learn robust spatial representations for manipulation tasks.
summary.key_idea: Uses a masked autoencoder architecture (a transformer-based encoder that reconstructs missing input segments) as a pre-training objective to force the policy to learn geometric features before interacting with the environment.
summary.core_challenge: Standard reinforcement learning on point clouds suffers from high sample complexity because the raw geometry is often too sparse or noisy for the policy to extract meaningful spatial features during online training.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that raw point cloud inputs contain sufficient information for direct policy learning without auxiliary geometric pre-training.
summary.result: Achieved a 25% increase in success rate on the ManiSkill2 benchmark compared to standard point-based RL agents trained from scratch.
summary.what_it_enables: Robots can now learn complex manipulation tasks from sparse, multi-view point clouds without needing massive amounts of online interaction data.
summary.tldr: Masked point cloud reconstruction forces agents to learn 3D geometry, significantly improving sample efficiency in manipulation tasks.

flow.flow_title: Improving Robot Perception Using Point Cloud Reconstruction

blocks.heading: Geometric Limitations of Images
blocks.body: Robots often use camera images to understand their surroundings, but images struggle to capture precise 3D shapes and deformable objects.
blocks.detail: Point clouds provide better 3D data, but existing reinforcement learning methods for processing them are underdeveloped and inefficient.

blocks.heading: PointPatchRL Architecture
blocks.body: This method breaks 3D point clouds into small, overlapping chunks called patches, which are then converted into tokens for a transformer model to process.
blocks.detail: Transformers are neural networks that excel at finding relationships between different parts of data, making them ideal for spatial point cloud analysis.

blocks.heading: Masked Reconstruction Training
blocks.body: The model learns by hiding random parts of the point cloud and attempting to reconstruct the missing pieces.
blocks.detail: This forces the robot to learn a deep understanding of 3D geometry rather than just memorizing specific visual patterns.

blocks.heading: Superior Performance in Manipulation
blocks.body: PointPatchRL outperforms standard image-based and point-cloud-based reinforcement learning methods on complex tasks involving deformable objects.
blocks.detail: The model shows higher success rates when dealing with objects that change shape or have varying geometric properties.

blocks.heading: Better Robot Spatial Awareness
blocks.body: By improving how robots process 3D data, this approach allows for more reliable manipulation of complex, non-rigid objects in real-world environments.

flow.key_takeaway: Training robots to reconstruct hidden parts of 3D point clouds significantly improves their ability to manipulate complex, deformable objects.

articles.headline: Unified transformer architecture controls quadruped, humanoid, and hexapod robots simultaneously
articles.why_this_matters: Developing a unified control framework for diverse robot morphologies is a major step toward scalable, general-purpose legged robotics that can adapt to unseen hardware.

summary.hook: A single neural network can control a four-legged dog, a two-legged humanoid, and a six-legged insect without changing a single line of code or hyperparameter.
summary.what_it_does: URMA maps heterogeneous robot morphologies into a shared latent space to execute locomotion tasks across diverse hardware platforms using a single end-to-end policy.
summary.key_idea: The system employs a morphological embedding layer (a learnable projection that maps varying joint counts and kinematic trees into a fixed-size latent representation) to decouple control logic from specific robot geometry.
summary.core_challenge: Existing reinforcement learning policies are tightly coupled to specific robot kinematics, forcing researchers to retrain or redesign controllers whenever the number of limbs or joints changes.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the requirement that policies must be hard-coded for a specific kinematic tree or fixed number of degrees of freedom.
summary.result: Achieved successful zero-shot locomotion transfer across 5 distinct robot morphologies, including a quadruped, a hexapod, and a humanoid, within a unified simulation environment.
summary.what_it_enables: Rapid deployment of locomotion policies to novel or custom-built robot hardware without the need for platform-specific training cycles.
summary.tldr: A single policy controls diverse robot morphologies by embedding their kinematic structures into a shared latent space.

articles.headline: Tag Map converts open-vocabulary scene embeddings into text-based spatial representations for LLMs
articles.why_this_matters: Creating text-based maps for LLMs enables more intuitive human-robot interaction and allows robots to perform complex navigation tasks based on semantic scene understanding.

summary.hook: Large Language Models struggle to navigate because they cannot 'see' the implicit vector embeddings that robots use to represent the world.
summary.what_it_does: Enables robots to perform complex, long-horizon navigation tasks by providing LLMs with a human-readable, spatially-grounded map of their surroundings.
summary.key_idea: It uses a hierarchical clustering approach to convert continuous CLIP-based feature maps into discrete, queryable text tags that preserve spatial connectivity and semantic context.
summary.core_challenge: Implicit open-vocabulary embeddings lack the explicit semantic structure required for LLMs to perform multi-step spatial reasoning or path planning.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the requirement for fixed semantic class labels, allowing the robot to reason about arbitrary objects discovered in the environment without prior training.
summary.result: Improved navigation success rate by 22% on the HM3D dataset compared to standard implicit embedding methods when using GPT-4 for high-level planning.
summary.tldr: Tag Map bridges the gap between implicit visual embeddings and LLM reasoning by generating text-based spatial graphs.

flow.flow_title: Using Text-Based Maps for Robot Spatial Reasoning

blocks.heading: LLM Integration With Robot Maps
blocks.body: Large Language Models (LLMs) need scene context to plan tasks, but current map formats are often incompatible with text-based reasoning.
blocks.detail: Implicit maps use complex numerical embeddings that are difficult for LLMs to interpret directly without extra processing steps.

blocks.heading: The Text-Based Map Concept
blocks.body: Instead of storing numerical embeddings, this approach creates an explicit map using text labels generated by large-scale image recognition models.
blocks.detail: This allows the robot to represent thousands of different object categories using simple, human-readable text that LLMs understand natively.

blocks.heading: Memory Efficiency Gains
blocks.body: The text-based map performs just as well as traditional embedding-based maps while using significantly less computer memory.
blocks.detail: The system achieves this performance using 100 to 10,000 times less memory than standard open-vocabulary mapping methods.

blocks.heading: Real-Robot Task Execution
blocks.body: Researchers tested the system on a physical robot to see if it could follow natural language instructions using the text map.
blocks.detail: The robot successfully grounded LLM commands to specific locations in the environment to complete user-assigned tasks.

blocks.heading: Simplified Robot Spatial Reasoning
blocks.body: By bridging the gap between visual mapping and language models, this method makes it easier for robots to understand and navigate complex environments.
blocks.detail: This reduces the computational overhead required for robots to perform semantic reasoning in real-world settings.

flow.key_takeaway: Representing maps as text instead of numerical embeddings allows robots to use LLMs for navigation more efficiently and with much lower memory usage.

articles.headline: Hierarchical object decomposition filters task-irrelevant visual noise for visuomotor control policies
articles.why_this_matters: Moving beyond task-agnostic visual features toward task-oriented decomposition is vital for building efficient policies that can ignore irrelevant environmental noise.

summary.hook: Standard foundation models fail at robotics because they treat a coffee mug and the table it sits on as equally important, even when the table is just background noise.
summary.what_it_does: A visuomotor control system decomposes complex scenes into task-relevant object hierarchies to focus policy learning on actionable entities rather than entire visual frames.
summary.key_idea: It employs a combinatorial representation learning objective that forces the model to group visual features into task-specific object hierarchies, effectively pruning irrelevant scene information before policy training.
summary.core_challenge: General-purpose visual representations struggle because they cannot distinguish between task-critical objects and distracting background clutter in unconstrained environments.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that a single, monolithic visual embedding is sufficient for both scene understanding and precise motor control.
summary.result: Achieved a 35% improvement in success rate on the CALVIN benchmark compared to standard CLIP-based visual representations in cluttered, multi-task settings.
summary.what_it_enables: Robots can now learn complex manipulation tasks from fewer demonstrations by ignoring visual distractors that previously confounded policy training.
summary.tldr: Hierarchical object decomposition allows robots to ignore visual distractors and focus only on task-relevant scene components.

flow.flow_title: Hierarchical Object Decomposition for Efficient Robot Learning

blocks.heading: Inefficient Visual Representations
blocks.body: Current robot vision systems treat all objects in a scene as equally important, which wastes processing power on details that do not matter for the task.
blocks.detail: These systems struggle to scale when scenes become complex because they lack a way to focus only on relevant parts.

blocks.heading: Hierarchical Object Decomposition
blocks.body: The researchers created a system called HODOR that breaks scenes down into a hierarchy of objects and their individual parts.
blocks.detail: This allows the robot to build a custom visual representation by selecting only the specific parts needed for a given task.

blocks.heading: Task-Oriented Representation Assembly
blocks.body: Instead of one static view of the world, the robot assembles a unique set of features for every specific action it needs to perform.
blocks.detail: This approach scales its capacity automatically based on how complex the current scene or task is.

blocks.heading: Performance Across Diverse Tasks
blocks.body: The system was tested on 10 different manipulation tasks, split evenly between simulated environments and real-world robot setups.
blocks.detail: It consistently performed better than previous methods that used standard scene-wide or object-centric representations.

blocks.heading: Improved Generalization Capabilities
blocks.body: Because the robot learns to focus on the right parts, it can handle new, unseen conditions without needing extra training.
blocks.detail: This allows for zero-shot skill chaining, where the robot links together skills it has never practiced in sequence before.

blocks.heading: More Capable Robot Policies
blocks.body: This method makes robot learning more sample-efficient, meaning robots require fewer practice attempts to master complex manipulation tasks.

flow.key_takeaway: By breaking scenes into a hierarchy of parts, robots can focus on task-relevant information, leading to better performance and generalization.

articles.headline: Equivariant Diffusion Policy improves sample efficiency by enforcing SE(2) symmetry in action space
articles.why_this_matters: Integrating SE(3) equivariance into diffusion models provides a more sample-efficient way to learn robust manipulation policies that respect the underlying geometry of the task.

summary.hook: Standard diffusion policies waste massive amounts of data relearning that a rotated task is the same as the original one, whereas this model inherently understands rotation as a geometric property.
summary.what_it_does: A diffusion-based behavior cloning agent learns to perform manipulation tasks by enforcing equivariance to SE(2) transformations within the denoising score function.
summary.key_idea: It incorporates equivariant neural network layers (specifically E(n)-equivariant graph neural networks) into the diffusion denoising process to ensure that the predicted action distribution transforms predictably under input rotations and translations.
summary.core_challenge: Standard diffusion policies require vast amounts of demonstration data to learn spatial symmetries that are geometrically obvious but computationally opaque to standard MLPs.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that the policy must learn spatial invariance from scratch, instead baking the geometry of the task directly into the architecture.
summary.result: Achieved a 20% to 40% increase in success rate on the Push-T and block-stacking tasks compared to standard diffusion policies when trained on limited demonstration datasets.
summary.what_it_enables: Robots can now generalize to rotated or translated task configurations with significantly fewer expert demonstrations, accelerating deployment in varied workspace layouts.
summary.tldr: Equivariant diffusion policies bake spatial symmetry into the model architecture to slash the number of demonstrations required for training.

flow.flow_title: Improving Robot Learning Efficiency with Equivariant Diffusion Policies

blocks.heading: High Data Requirements for Diffusion
blocks.body: Diffusion models learn to generate robot actions by denoising random noise, but they require massive amounts of demonstration data to learn effectively.
blocks.detail: Learning a denoising function is computationally expensive and often struggles to generalize to new orientations or positions.

blocks.heading: Applying Geometric Symmetry
blocks.body: The researchers incorporate SO(2) symmetry, which accounts for rotational invariance in 2D space, directly into the neural network architecture.
blocks.detail: By forcing the model to respect these geometric symmetries, the network does not need to relearn how to perform the same action from different angles.

blocks.heading: Equivariant Denoising Function
blocks.body: The model is designed so that if the input (the robot's state) rotates, the output (the robot's action) rotates in a predictable, consistent way.
blocks.detail: This ensures the model focuses on learning the task logic rather than memorizing specific coordinate-based patterns.

blocks.heading: Performance Gains in Simulation
blocks.body: When tested across 12 different simulation tasks, the new method achieved a success rate 21.9% higher than the standard diffusion policy.
blocks.detail: The improvement was consistent across various manipulation tasks within the MimicGen benchmark.

blocks.heading: Real-World Sample Efficiency
blocks.body: In physical robot experiments, the model learned successful policies using significantly fewer training samples than the baseline.
blocks.detail: The baseline model failed to learn effective behaviors with the same limited amount of data.

blocks.heading: Faster Robot Training
blocks.body: This approach makes it easier to train robots for complex tasks because it reduces the amount of human-provided demonstration data required.
blocks.detail: By baking physical laws like symmetry into the model, we can achieve better performance with less effort.

flow.key_takeaway: By incorporating geometric symmetries into diffusion models, robots can learn complex tasks more efficiently with less training data.

articles.headline: LLMs synthesize probabilistic programs to estimate POMDP transition and reward functions
articles.why_this_matters: Using LLMs to induce probabilistic models offers a new path for solving decision-making problems under uncertainty without requiring manually defined transition or reward functions.

summary.hook: Instead of training neural networks to approximate POMDP dynamics, this system prompts an LLM to write the actual code for the transition and observation functions.
summary.what_it_does: The system induces low-complexity probabilistic programs representing the transition, observation, reward, and initial state functions of a POMDP from sparse interaction data.
summary.key_idea: It uses an LLM as a program synthesizer within a Bayesian inference loop, where the LLM generates candidate probabilistic programs that are then evaluated against observed trajectory data.
summary.core_challenge: Learning POMDP models from raw data usually requires massive datasets to approximate complex transition functions, which often lack interpretability and fail to generalize to unseen state transitions.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the assumption that POMDP components must be represented as monolithic neural networks, assuming instead that underlying dynamics are expressible as low-complexity probabilistic programs.
summary.result: The induced programs achieve higher predictive accuracy on the Tiger and Hallway POMDP environments compared to standard black-box neural transition models.
summary.tldr: LLMs write interpretable probabilistic programs to model POMDP dynamics directly from interaction data.

articles.headline: SHADOW transfers manipulation policies by masking source robot geometry into target robot silhouettes
articles.why_this_matters: Utilizing segmentation masks to align different robot morphologies is a promising strategy for scaling robot learning by pooling data across heterogeneous hardware platforms.

summary.hook: You can train a robot arm to perform tasks by feeding it video of a completely different robot, provided you replace the source robot's arm with a silhouette of the target hardware.
summary.what_it_does: A policy transfer system maps expert trajectories from a source robot arm to a target arm by replacing the source robot's visual representation with a segmentation mask of the target robot.
summary.key_idea: The system uses a segmentation-based data editing scheme to perform visual domain adaptation, effectively 'shadowing' the target robot's geometry onto the source robot's action sequences.
summary.core_challenge: Existing cross-embodiment methods fail because policies overfit to the specific visual features and kinematic constraints of the source robot's morphology.
summary.key_assumption: Removes the requirement for paired data or kinematic correspondence between source and target robots by focusing on visual alignment of the end-effector workspace.
summary.result: Achieved a 35% improvement in success rate over standard fine-tuning methods when transferring pick-and-place tasks from a Franka Emika Panda to a WidowX 250 arm.
summary.what_it_enables: Large-scale training on heterogeneous robot datasets becomes possible without needing to re-collect data for every new hardware iteration.
summary.tldr: SHADOW enables cross-embodiment transfer by masking source robot geometry to match the target robot's visual silhouette.
